item.title: Stupid human tricks
item.description:
Graceful dismount.
Ended as well as it could have!
The engagement
#Scorpion
I need this picnic table!
Both were at fault here
Safety Second!
Splort!
Russian hospitality #NSFWstyle
It’s Batgirl
I suspect this is in Minnesota, where I live
I think she’s gonna need a trip to the dentist
This one is very old, I’ve seen it many times, but it still makes me laugh. I’ve gotten my belt loop hooked on the dresser drawer handle more times than I’d like to admit.
The cop says “Nope, just leave her alone, let her go. She got this.”
I am certain they climbed over several warning signs on the way down. Doggie tried to warn them!
I honestly thought she was gonna lose an eye
This falls under the “hold my Cosmo” category
I upvote all comments for the first 72 hrs.

item.dc:creator: tuyehbhi

item.title: WHOLESOME Saturday Dump of Nice things to warm your heart
item.description:
1. He's always been cool
2. Building a tiny treadmill for a tiny hamster
3. Scratchies disable dog
4. Mlem
5. Wingman
6
7. Awwww
8. Damn that is exquisite
9. Happy doggo
10. Husband surprises bride by flying her parents for the wedding
11. You, you... you stop scrolling and listen for this lady for a sec
12
13
14. I need to find that store
15. Today I wanted to share with you that I started reading this awesome book called 'Blood, Sweat & Pixels'. It's about the history of various indie and mainstream games and the struggle of the devs to get them done. I have to say it's very inspiring and it helps me on my own journey as a game dev. To be honest, I've been working on the same game for almost 18 months now and it can get a bit frustrating. Imagine having to hold your focus on one thing for so long (while juggling everyday life). However this book inspired me and helped me re-start my passion. Anyway, if you want to support the game, a simple wishlist would mean the world to me. Please check it out on Steam: https://store.steampowered.com/app/2336120/Dont_Press_The_Button_To_Delete_The_Multiverse_The_Game/ P.S. 1. This here is a plushie duck that I'm hoping a lot of players will develop affection for. P.S. 2HAVE A GREAT AND SUNNY WEEKEND, FRIENDOS!

item.title: I love that he did the math of how much was owed before he responded with biting sarcasm because no way was he going to go back and asking is just insanity.
item.description: In August 1865, Jordan Anderson, a former slave, received a letter from his old master, Colonel P.H. Anderson, asking him to return to the Tennessee plantation to help restore it after the Civil War. Jordan, who had been freed by Union soldiers in 1864, was living in Dayton, Ohio, with his family. In response, he dictated a letter through his abolitionist employer, Valentine Winters, which was published in the Cincinnati Commercial. The letter humorously and pointedly requested 52 years' worth of back pay for himself and his wife, totaling $11,680, as a condition for his return. This amount was calculated based on $25 a month for 32 years for Jordan and $2 a week for 20 years for his wife, Mandy. Jordan's letter not only highlighted the absurdity of his former master's request but also underscored the harsh realities and injustices of slavery. He detailed his improved life in Ohio, where he earned a respectable wage, his children attended school, and his family was treated with dignity. The letter's deadpan humor and sharp wit made it a powerful piece of social commentary, illustrating the resilience and intelligence of former slaves. It remains a significant historical document, offering insight into the experiences and perspectives during the Reconstruction era.
item.dc:creator: Joyika

item.title: For a friend, for you
item.description:
Just like it says: I put together a package for a friend on all the basics for critical thinking, and thought other imgurians might be interested in it for themselves, or one of their friends. Please enjoy with my compliments...
It's all well and good to think critically, but you'll need some tools to do it. First, we need to evaluate what we are being presented...
That's great, but, applying critical thinking back on itself, what are some of the ways critical thinking can go awry? The biggest culprit: Biases.
Biases continue below
I hope you've found this either informative, useful to make your own gift for someone in your life, or simply useful as a resource.
item.dc:creator: ThePsychAutist

item.title: The radar system protecting America (and Canada too!)
item.description:
In the far north of Canada and the US, in some of the most remote locations on the planet, is a chain of radar stations with one job: to detect an oncoming attack from over the north pole. But how did that chain get built? And when? Let's find out?
Historical precedent: Chain Home radar line, United Kingdom (WWII) Before WWII, the British government quietly constructed a chain of radar stations in the south of their country. The new technology would prove vital in the war to come, detecting incoming German bombers with enough time to scramble defending fighters. In their turn the Germans built their own radar defences that allowed the Luftwaffe to target Allied bombers, often exacting horrific casualties.
1940s - 1950s: The Cold War Soon after the end of WWII, as it began to look as if the next big conflict would be with the Soviet Union, American and Canadian military planners decided it would make sense to, first, create an integrated North American defence network, and build a chain of radar stations modelled on the radar networks built by the British and Germans during the war. Of course, the scale of the North American version was vastly more challenging in terms of size and terrain. The first attempt was the Pine Tree radar line, mostly along the US-Canada border. The most likely air attack route from the USSR would have been over the north pole, so the radar was built for that. Unfortunately, the tech for this first line wasn't great; they had a lot of trouble making it work properly. Plus as jet technology developed quickly, it became apparent they'd need more lead time, so they decided to build another line further north.
The Mid-Canada line was built to supplement the Pine Tree line. Built mostly in the northern areas of Canada's western provinces as well as northern Ontario and Quebec, it was even more challenging to build and operate. With stations built in remote, inaccessible areas unconnected by roads, keeping them supplied was a massive (and expensive) undertaking. Yet it was still a very flawed system that didn't work very well. Luckily, advancing technology allowed the construction of yet another chain of radar stations even further north.
The Distant Early Warning or DEW line was an extraordinary feat of technology and constructions. Running several hundred kilometres north of the arctic circle, most of it ran along the shore of the Arctic Ocean, then along Baffin Island and northern Greenland (the station pictured here was in Greenland). This radar line was around 3,000 miles long (just under 5,000km), and was built in some of the harshest conditions the planet has to offer. The stations could only be supplied by air in wintertime, requiring an even larger continent-spanning logistical network. For a time it was supplemented by seaborne radar picket vessels in the Pacific and Atlantic Oceans, keeping North America snugly wrapped in a protective radar shield that never slept. Unfortunately, by the time it was finished in the late 1950s, the DEW line was already obsolete. Why? Because advancing technology meant the emerging threat of Soviet attack came from ICBMS -- Intercontinental Ballistic Missiles. They still flew over the pole, but moved so fast that not only would they be incredibly hard to detect, but the time to react would be so limited that literally every second would count. The answer? To build yet another chain of radar stations.
The good news for builders of BMEWS -- Ballistic Missile Early Warning System -- was that it would only require the construction of three radar stations, in Alaska, Greenland and the UK. The bad news was that building a vast network capable of detecting and tracking a mass nuclear missile attack by, potentially, thousands of incoming missiles, with zero time for analysis and double-checking (total flight time of a Soviet ICBM from launch to impact would be 10-30 minutes) would push the limits of detection and computing technology. They were able to make it work, but it was very tricky, often triggered by innocent phenomena such as flocks of birds or even a moonrise. Any one of these mistakes might easily have triggered a mistaken nuclear retaliation attack by the US military, but somehow humanity avoided that catastrophe.
The network was run by NORAD -- the North American Aerospace Defence Command. This is a military structure headquartered at Colorado Springs, Colorado. It was, and remains, a joint Canadian-US military command. They never sleep, keeping an eye on all potential threats -- and ready to activate nuclear retaliation. Starting in the 1960s, the radar lines were supplemented by satellite systems equipped to detect missile launches and nuclear explosions. The DEW line and its predecessors were shut down after the Cold War ended, being replaced by the Solid State Phased Array Radar System (SSPARS) in 2001. This uses stations located at the old BMEWS sites, in addition to some new ones. North America is still wrapped snug in a protective radar blanket.
The Soviets never were able to build a comparable radar chain; they simply didn't have the resources. Some radar sites were quite notable, however, the most well-known, codenamed Duga, being the source of an annoying, repeating electromagnetic signal nicknamed the "Russian Woodpecker." Radio manufacturers literally had to design "woodpecker blankers" in their circuit boards to block it out. The site located near the former Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant still stands, but has been inactive for decades. It is the site of avid urban explorers, It really is a huge structure, around 150 metres (around 500 feet) high. The Soviets tried to "outflank" the BMEWS system by developing a FOBS missile technology -- Fractional Orbital Bombardment System. These missiles, instead of flying over the north pole, would briefly enter orbit, fly over the south pole, and attack the US from its unguarded south flank. However the FOBS system required much smaller warheads, and were made obsolete by the American addition of satellite tracking to its detection systems. Plus there were still nuclear submarines, which could launch nukes with flight time of only 5-10 minutes.
Most of the shut-down radar sites have been dismantled. And of course they required a massive clean-up effort costing around half a billion dollars. But there are still a few that are still standing, abandoned, and you can visit them -- if you can get to them.
item.dc:creator: Huor

item.title: I guess I know this song
item.dc:creator: Fulustreka

item.title: One Take - John Landis and Tim Matheson were just off screen tossing the items at him.
item.description: Comedy, especially physical comedy, often plays quite well if you can get it to play with as few cuts as possible. If you can do it in one shot, even better. During the scene in the grocery store where Flounder is tasked with catching assorted items being thrown his way, Landis and Tim Matheson were just off screen tossing the items at him. Landis did not necessarily expect Stephen Furst to nail it, but he managed to catch every item and crush the whole thing in a single take, much to the director’s amusement, and amazement.
item.dc:creator: Jbelkin

item.title: Lost cat walked 800 miles home!
item.description:
Almost made all the way! But how? Did have a map? Did he have a phone? Did he use the stars? Seriously how?
Rayne Beau

item.title: Swords Comic x Litterbox crossover! [OC]
item.description:
Matt from Swords Comic https://www.instagram.com/swordscomic got me good with this one!
The animated pilot is funded (THANK YOU!) but the more we raise the better it will be, so we've lined up a bunch of exclusive rewards that will only be available by backing! Check them out here: https://www.kickstarter.com/projects/litterboxanimation/pilot-episode?ref=f52qpp
item.dc:creator: LitterboxComics
